title: Say that it is a list
why.empty: The editor is empty. You deleted the page instead of making a list. Bring the code back.
why.not-closed:
A tag is open and never closed, or closed in the wrong order. In lists this happens easily — every
  • wants its
    • .
hint2.not-closed:
Count the opening
  • tags and the closing
    • tags. The numbers must match.
hint3.not-closed:
The
    closes too. It wraps everything — so the
goes last, after the final .
hint4.not-closed:
Four things means four
  • and four
    • . Plus one
      and one
    around them.
why.no-li: There are not four things in a list. Each of the four gets wrapped in an <li>, and all of them together in a <ul>.
hint2.no-li: Two parts: <ul> on the outside, <li> around each thing inside.
hint3.no-li:
• Bread
  • Milk
— one list, two things. One wrapper, as many things as you like.
hint4.no-li: Wrap the frame, the front wheel, the rear wheel and the bell — each in its own <li>. Then wrap all four in a <ul>.
why.empty-li: There is an empty <li>. An empty item is not an item — it says nothing. Either put text in it or remove it.
hint2.empty-li: You opened and closed an <li> with nothing between them.
hint3.empty-li:
• Frame — the tag is empty and Frame hangs outside it. It should be
  • Frame
    • .
why.old-mess:
The old
tags are still there. You do not need them — every
  • is a block and takes its own line. Delete the old paragraph with the dashes.
hint2.old-mess:
Search for
in your code. Not one should remain.
hint3.old-mess: You are not adding a list NEXT TO the mess. You are replacing the mess with a list.
hint4.old-mess:
Remove the whole

with the dashes and the
tags. The

    goes in its place.
why.lost-items: Something is missing from the list. The four are: frame, front wheel, rear wheel, bell. The task is to organise them, not to shorten them.
hint2.lost-items: Count the items in the result. There should be four.
why.manual-dashes: You kept the dashes. You do not need them — the browser draws the bullets. Now your dashes sit NEXT TO its bullets.
hint2.manual-dashes: Look at the result on the right. Every line has two marks in front of it. You only asked for one.
hint3.manual-dashes:
• - Frame
    • — the dash is part of the text, and the bullet comes from the browser. The two add up.
hint4.manual-dashes: Remove every dash from inside the <li>. Only the name of the thing stays.
blocks.0.text: The problem from the last lesson: you drew a list instead of making one. Here is the tool.
blocks.3.text: <ul> wraps the whole list. It comes from "unordered list". Meaning the order does not matter: the frame is not "more first" than the bell.
blocks.4.text: <li> is each thing inside. From "list item".
blocks.6.text: The dashes are gone. You did not type a single bullet — the browser draws them itself. You only said what this IS; how it looks, it decided.
blocks.7.text:
And there is no
. Every
  • is a block — it takes its own line. It does not need to be told.
blocks.8.text: And if the order does matter
blocks.9.text: Then it is not <ul> but <ol> — "ordered list". Steps in a recipe, a ranking, instructions. The browser numbers them itself, and if you swap two — the numbers rearrange on their own. Try that with hand-typed digits.
